Issue No.l6 • November 2012
7 Badass E:mail Lessons
(You Can Profit From IDlDlediately)
From A Punk Rock Legend
What up...
I'm a BIG fan of the punk rock band "Social Distortion". I've been a big fan for
many years and if I ever get to meet Mike Ness (the band founder and leader) I'm going
to ask him to autograph my ballz (I,ve always wanted to say that ha). Anyway they kick
assimus. And over the past several months, I've been listening to their stuff while writing
emails and ads, etc. And weird as it sounds, it was not long after I started doing that when
I noticed writing getting... easier. In other words, my emails got tighter, more interesting,
and had more "heart." (If you catch my drift ...)
One day I finally figured out why:
Social Distortion lyrics are basically email "templates."
Yes ... you could say this about a lot of bands and songs. But Social Distortion music
is especially worthy of study if you want to write money making emails that get people to
buy your products and services. Mike Ness (who I am pretty sure writes most of the songs,
except for the cover songs) creates these VERY simple lyrics that are also kind of deep, at
the same time. There,s the surface stuff and then there,s the stuff between the lines. On
-1-

-- 1 of 25 --

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
the surface he is often giving warnings learned from his own mistakes, his own flaws, and
his own inner demons. And between the lines, you're getting what many of us fans would
call "ballads of human nature, life and loss." They get you thinking and, sometimes, even
behaving differently. Fact is, Mike Ness can not only WRITE a song, but he can
PRESENT it in a way that's fun, easy on the mind, and instantly understandable.
JUST like a good eDlail.
Heck, just like ANY good piece of copy.
And in my humble (but accurate) opinion, you cannot do it any better, cleaner or
more ballzier than Social Distortion. Which, again, makes their songs great email
templates. Anyway, you'd have to read or listen to the lyrics to understand what I'm
talking about. I'll give you a lead on some good ones to look up in a minute. But first I
want to break down some of what's going on in these lyrics that you can apply to your
emails. Unless you've been writing a crap load of emails, much of this you'll have to
consciously apply. But the more you do it, the more automatic it'll get -- you just gotta get
up and crank out that email each day.
Alright, enough of this "man-crush" gushing. Here are some of the big lessons to
extract from Social D lyrics:
1. Silnple, yet powerful writing:
A lot of people try to get WAY too deep in their emails. They use way too many big
words and heavy ideas. Some of that is okay, but it's got to be like using a spice. You don't
DUMP the entire bottle of whatever spice you're using into the sauce. You put a pinch or
two, enough to add flavor but not overwhelm the sauce itself. And so it is with email
writing. Social Distortion lyrics do this perfectly. They might throw in something deep.
But the focus is on the simplicity, understanding what's being said and being able to follow
the thoughts from beginning to the end. Email is similar to copywriting in that you want
simple as Gene Schwartz said "move gut" copy -- copy people understand immediately at
the "gut" level. This is Social Distortion lyrics. And what's funny is, when you write
enough emails (you ARE writing daily now, right?) your subconscious will start spitting
out kinda profound thoughts and ideas making you think, "huh, where did THAT come
from?" You listen to Social Distortion and it's all very simple ideas and sounds... and then
they're throw in something like, "You can run all your life But not go anywhere" or "How
-2-
www.BcnSettle.com 	• 	Copyright 2012 Ben Settle, All Rights ReseJVed 	• 	Published By Settle, LLC
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 2 of 25 --

THE 	EMAIL PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
can you love me if you don't love yourself" or ''Yeah, good times come and good times go
I only wish the good times would last a little longer" -- these aren't super deep or
anything, but they are easy to relate to, and strike at our own sense of nostalrja, lost
opportunities, and inner angsts. Emails that do this can get very high repeat readership as
it nourishes your readers' brains in ways the typical emails marketers are sending never
will.
2. Storytelling:
The stories in a lot of these Social Distortion songs (especially "Story Of My Life" a
great template for emails) flow very smoothly, in a way that's easy to follow and that has
both emotion and depth. Again, not TOO much. But really if you just tell a story about
something that happened to you or a customer or someone in your market who has the
problem your list wants solved (this is the ideal kind of story to tell), this is automatic. Just
identifying with the problem in the story creates the right mix of emotion. You don't have
to "try" to insert it in there, it's just ... there. Example: If you sell a product that shows dog
owners how to get their dog to stop crapping on the carpet, and tell a story about a guy
who had expensive carpet and his dog was staining it constantly costing him a ton of
money. .. that emotion is built-in. It's the same with Social D lyrics. In fact, a lot of them
are true cautionary tales. And amigo, there is no greater service you can give your list (or
anyone else, for that matter) than a cautionary tale. It's the ultimate in soft teaching vs
hard teaching. It doesn't reveal anything about what you teach in your product, yet does
them a service, builds your value up and, in 9 out of 10 cases, they will want your product
to help them prevent the very danger you're cautioning them about as a result. (Even if
they only want to have your product to feel "safe" -- yes, we human beings are weird like
that. .. )
3. Rhytlun & flow:
If there's one big complaint "critics" have of Social Distortion, it's that their lyrics
(and sound) are a bit too repetitious. That may very well be true, but it's part of what
makes them WORK, even if it's not very artsy. Listen up, listen good and never forget
what I'm about to say: Repetition is GOOD in emails. I hate using this word, but it sorta
fits ... but repetition is kinda hypnotic. It keeps people focused on your message and is
harder to pull away from than non-repetitious ideas, words, thoughts, etc. I remember
Matt Furey saying in his original email course (from 2005, I have no idea how to find it
anymore, so don't ask) talking about this. And until then I'd never really thought about it.
-3-

But his example was guys like Winston Churchhill. He'd get on the radio and say things
like:
''We shall fight on the beaches~ we shall fight on the landing
grounds~ we shall fight in the fields and in the streets~ we shall
fight in the hills... "
Note that constant repetition "we shall fight". The great orators have always done
this going back hundreds (probably thousands) of years. And this is awesome in emails,
too. Eugene Schwartz also spaketh about this in his great copywriting tome
"Breakthrough Advertising". Although he was talking more about repeating words and
ideas that you've proven in your copy to keep building belie( It's all the same -- repetition,
Repetition, REPETITION, son. Social D does it, and so, I say, shalt you! Here's another
example of using repetition I used a few months ago:
The loser says "The more emails I 	send, 	the more unsubscribes I 	get"
The winner says "The more emails I 	send, 	the more sales I 	get"
The loser says "This email got a 45% open rate"
The winner says "This email brought in $4,500 profit"
The loser says "I want to be a great writer"
The winner says "I want to be a great marketer"
The loser says "I'll do what my favorite goo-roo says based on blind
faith"
The winner says "I'll do things MY way based on what experience has
taught me"
The loser says, 	"I'll 	•reverse engineer' 	what others are doing and skip
having to learn via investment of money, 	time and sweat"
The winner says, 	"I'll invest money, time and sweat to learn my craft"
The loser says, 	"I'm going to write my ads with a swipe file"
The winner says, 	"I'm going to write my ads based on my market intel"
The loser says, 	"I have read over 100 marketing books 1 time"
The winner says, 	"I have read the 10 best marketing books 10 times"
The loser says, 	"I want to 	•manifest' 	money by giving away lots of free
information"
The winner says, 	"I'll find out what pain my market has, 	and sell them
the solution in a way they want to be sold by"

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
Again, you must do this consciously (conscious competence) at first. But eventually,
it'll be natural. And while you won't do it in EVERY email, you'll find your brain putting
these repetitions down when it makes sense.
4. Emotional but not whiney:
You know with a lot of music (especially these days where society pretty much tries
to castrate men at birth) a lot of music is either too hard/ cold... or it's way too whiney. It's
hard for a lot of guy bands to pull off writing lyrics without sounding like a bunch of
whiney little pussies (hey, let's call it what it is) or sounding like sociopathic douche bags .
Not Social Distortion. They give you that happy medium where they're telling you
cautionary tales of pain, anger, heart break and failure ... but in a way where it's not
whiney in the least. Again, cautionary tales. It's not "oh woe is me!" it's "hey, I screwed
up, don't make the same dumb mistake." And emails should (mostly, depending on what
market you're selling to, there ARE exceptions to this-- such as if you're writing to weight
loss, for example) be like this as well. Go ahead and get their emotions going, reveal
something painful or humiliating. But don't whine about it. Don't look for sympathy. And
don't try to get people to feel sorry for you (by the way, do you see how these last 3
sentences were REPETITIOUS "don't. .. don't ... don't. .. " that's how it's done, boss).
What you want to do is be an authority -- a leader -- someone who gets your message
across without sounding like a crybaby which turns people off (think about certain TV
evangelists who sit there and cry the whole time, like a bunch of pansies, and they don't
last long, I've noticed ...)
5. Voice matches the message:
Very few people think about this, but luckily your pal Ben Settle does. A lot of times,
people try to sound tough or whatever in their emails when they're selling something that
needs a softer touch. Or, conversely, people have too soft of a voice when selling
something that requires a bit more toughness. Example: My friend john Angel was telling
me how he knew this guy who was a john Carlton fanboy (most of us in copywriting are
John Carlton fanboys, no crime there...) But this dude was selling something like salsa
dancing. So his copy was saying things like "Vicious head turns!" and things of that
nature-- as if writing to wannabe alpha males learning martial arts (no doubt the john
Carlton ad copy the fanboy was reading and modeling) instead of women wanting to
learn salsa dancing. This happens a lot in emails too. Social Distortion has a very
-5-
www.BcnSettlc.com 	• 	Copyright 20 12 Ben Settle, All Rights ReseJVed 	• 	Published By Settle, LLC
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 25 --

THE 	EMAIL PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
congruent tone and voice (hell, Mike Ness's voice SOUNDS like he's a punk, which is
perfect for punk rock). And so should your emails. You can use your own personality (and
you should). But make sure you're talking to your market in a tone they want. When I
write emails for female weight loss, I don't use terminology I use to BenSettle.com
subscribers. When I write emails to baby boomer men with prostate problems, I don't talk
to them using GenX slang. This is probably not something many people reading this issue
have a problem with. But it's worth nothing just in case...
6. Ballziness:
This won't apply to everyone as far as a lesson to learn from (see #5 above), but
there's an inherent "ballziness" to the lyrics. This takes themes that might normally be
kind of wimpy sounding and sappy... and gives them teeth. But it's not the kind like you
see from wannabe goo-roos today -- where people just try to talk tough in their emails and
copy, etc. Social D does it without trying to sound ballzy, it's just natural. You want to do
this with your emails as well (again, keep it in line with your market ...) One day I'm going
to include a list of "ballzy words" for emails in this newsletter. A sort of cheat sheet for
when you're looking for the right spice to toss into ye old email to give it a certain edge,
which makes emails far more fun and interesting and entertaining..
7. Contrast:
Finally, we talked about this in the hallowed pages of this newsletter a while back.
But high contrast gets big attention, big readership and, yes, big SALES (hey! another
example of repetition -- on a roll here, Bub...) Whenever you can insert contrasting words,
ideas, phrases, paragraph/ sentence lengths, or anything else into your market, the better.
Frankly, you can take just about any topic (no matter how dry or dull) and make it
interesting by using high contrast throughout the email. I notice Mike Ness does this in a
lot of his songs: "Painful kiss", "outlaw love song'' "there must be a heaven because I've
already done my time in hell" -- and the list goes on. Lots and lots of contrast JDakes
everything you write autom.atically m.ore interesting, autom.atically m.ore
fun to read, and autom.atically m.ore profitable (again with the repetition ...)
And that's a wrap on that. Now your homework assignment young Solomon is to get
ye to Google, find some of Social Distortion's song lyrics Gust type in "social distortion
lyrics" and you'll find them all), and read them. Get inspired by them (it's even better if
you go to YouTube and listen, but reading the lyrics is fine -- especially if you don't like

their style of music). And let them influence your emails. Become a "Mike Ness email
writer" and I believe your sales will go up dramatically over time (as mine have). A few
song lyrics to get you started include:
• 	Story of my life King of fools
• 	lwaswrong
• 	Ball and chain
• 	King of fools
• 	When the angels sing
• 	Bad Luck
When reading through them, note the repetition of ideas, words, themes... the easy
flowing (short) stories... the impactful (without being heavy) concepts. It's just good
"badass" (how's that for contrast?) email writing. And not only will your customers
appreciate reading your emails... but they'll soon love buying from them as well...
Speaking Of Badass ...
Below may be the single most badass email you'll ever read. It's got EVERYTHING
a profitable email needs to educate (softly), entertain and inspire people to buy. And by
the way, I learn FAR more studying non-sales oriented emails (i.e. emails, blog posts,
articles, etc) that are not trying to sell me anything than I do from sales pitches. Why?
Because the pitching part is probably the easiest part for those of us who cut our teeth on
writing direct response sales letters. But it's the stuff that comes BEFORE the pitch and
that sets up the sales... and that builds interest and desire ... that's the real art of email
writing. Anyway, I'm in a mastermind group with "Email Players" subscriber Doberman
Dan Gallapoo (www.DobermanDan.com). And earlier this year he sent us an email that
he could (and subsequently did) use to sell almost anything. It would not matter much
what the product is (with some imagination). In fact, the more ads and sales letters and
emails I write ... the more I realize the product almost doesn't matter if you know your
market and how to get into someone's psychology (and this topic, my friend, could be a
whole newsletter issue itsel£ .. ) What matters is the story, the emotion, the suspense and
build up... that keeps you riveted. Realize people are going to spend their money on the
solution they're looking for. The only difference is, will they spend it on your product or
someone else's? Well, if you do what the following email does, I can virtually guarantee

you they'll spend it on YOUR product-- even if your "pitch" isn't very blatant. Anyway,

Subject Line: 	Turn your 	(or your friends) 	worst nightmares into emails
You guys wouldn't believe how my day started today at about 5:50 AM.
It involves a 230 lb man standing over my bed, 	followed by me racking
a round in my 12 gauge shotgun and pointing it at his chest.
And the rest is funny as hell.
HINT: 	Guess which of my 2 dogs saved our lives •••
It's 	just too darn good of a story to share by email though.
I'll share it on our next call.
CRUCIAL life & death lesson you should take from this:
If you don't have at LEAST one gun, 	loaded and at the ready ••• 	have
been trained to use it under high stress conditions 	(trained to handle
the "fight or flight" 	reaction ••• 	VERY important) •••
if you value your life and the life of your family, 	drop EVERYTHING
you're doing right this very minute and go get a gun. A 12 gauge
shotgun is a good start.
Then book a class at Tactical Response, 	Front Sight ••• or one of those
places with competent trainers that understand how to train people to
shoot under high stress situations.
Why?
When seconds count, 	the police are only minutes away.
I can tell you from HUNDREDS of incidents ••• 	the only thing the cops
are going to be able to do in situations like this is draw a chalk line
around your body, 	your wife's body and your children's bodies.
Your family's protection is YOUR responsibility ••• 	and if you're stupid
enough to depend on someone else for that ••• you deserve what will very
likely happen to you.
Rock onl

-- 8 of 25 --

THE 	EMAIL PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
Doberman "I'm glad I don't have to appear in front of the grand jury on
the use of 	justifiable deadly force this morning" Dan
That's one helluva an email, isn't it? And like I said, Dan could just as easily turned
that email into a pitch to sell virtually any kind of product (like firearm training or, with
some imagination, anything else that has to do with being prepared). That email is one of
the best I've ever seen and he didn't even try to make it a "good email." Which brings me
to the last point I want to make about it: Often the best emails are "off the cuff" -- where
you just start writing about something interesting, emotional, scary, funny, exciting, hair-
raising, etc. This is why I tell people who struggle to write emails to just pretend you're
writing to a friend who happens to be interested in solving the problem your product is
designed to help. Loosen up. Have some fun. And then send it out. The worst that
happens is... nobody buys! (I don't consider hate mail or complaints a bad thing, in fact if
you get some, then use it to help you sell more stuff the next day by addressing it and
turning the complaint into a reason to buy -- more on this in future "Email Players"
issues). So don't be afraid to start writing, tell a story (simplest way to write an email) and
let the hounds out.
You might be surprised by the results ...
The 	''Let Me Tell You A Story'' 	Opening
And on that note (telling a story) I want to show you a VERY simple yet effective
way to begin an email. A way that gets people naturally leaning in to read more, and
"preps" them for a fun ride. I call it the "let me tell you a story" opening. And it basically
means you start your email by literally saying, "Let me tell you a story." Again, I get most
of my inspiration from blogs, articles, etc that are NOT selling anything. And this comes
from a blog I read every day called "Captain Capitalism"
(www.captaincapitalism.blogspot.com).
Check out these mangoes:
Let me tell you a story.
Was at a bar/restaurant last night. Within the group of people I'm at
is a mother-son combination and a 	(frankly) 	overweight, 	ugly middle
aged woman 	(henceforth referred to as "the OUMAW").
-9-

-- 9 of 25 --

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
The mother and the OUMAW are tag teaming this poor boy about what to do
when it comes to matters of the ladies and courting when he heads off
to college/the real world. 	And all the advice is absolutely wrong,
only guaranteeing he'll be fed to the meat-grinder at a faster rate.
See how simple that was?
And, let's face it, if you have a cool story to tell, you don't need to get fancy with
how to open it. Just say "Let me tell you a story." You can also modify it and say "Let me
tell you a cool story" or "Let me tell you this life-or-death story" or any other way you
want to change it that fits the tone of the story you want to tell. Like I tell people, email is
NOT harcl. It's very simple. People only run into trouble when they try to complicate it.
(Incidentally, the way the above story is set up is PERFECT for email, too, you could
easily model it for virtually any kind of story you want to tell ... "I was talking with XYZ
and he/she was getting bad advice ... here's what I told him/her". Again, very simple... )
An Open Letter To Idiots:
How Mocking Your EneJDies
Can Make You More Sales
The "letter to ... " email is pretty cool, too. I actually wrote one of these not long ago
after being inspired by (yes ...) a Social Distortion song called "Dear Lover". The song is
like a letter to a lover who SPURNS him. And I thought, "huh, I could use that format to
write a letter to people who piss me off, too ... like Spelling Nazis... " and so I did (see
below). There's a lot of power in MOCKING (yes mocking!) your enemies. Even the old
Biblical prophets did this sort of thing-- it's very persuasive as long as you know you're
right and that whoever your mocking is doing harm to the people you're trying to help
(then it's your DUTY to mock the hell out of them, in my humble, but accurate,
opinion ... ). Anyway, here's an example (that pulled more sales than usual) you can
EASILY adapt for what you sell-- simply by writing an "open letter" to your enemies
about whatever it is they do that you want to mock. (You can probably even adapt the
way I segue into the sales pitch, it should work universally for most any kind of product/
service). Try it-- methinks your list will love reading it and, I won't lie to you, it's kind of
fun to write...
-10-

Subject Line: An open letter to all spelling Nazis
Dear Spelling Nazis,
You suck.
Nobody likes you.
And, 	in fact, 	I 	doubt you even like yourselves.
How could you?
Especially when your only bragging right is a worthless college degree
in literature and the ability to read an email and point out every
misplaced jot and tittle.- yet can't write anything that makes money to
save your own life?
It's actually kinda sad.
I 	SHOULD feel sorry for you.
But I 	don't.
In fact, 	I 	take great pleasure in mocking you.
Frankly, 	I 	sometimes think it MUST be an act.
It's like these Hollywood actors who spout off inane political nonsense
- 	they can't possibly be THAT stoopid, 	so maybe they're 	just trying to
impress their peers to get another acting gig.
But who are you trying to impress, 	spelling Nazi?
You're certainly not impressing customers.
They couldn't care less about a misspelling or botched attempt at
grammar.
Most of the time, 	they don't notice it.
(Unless they're a pathetic spelling Nazi, 	too.) 	If the ad/email is
written the right way 	(i.e. by someone who knows how to SELL and not
just SPELL) 	the customer is too caught up in the adventure to care.
It's no different than high grossing movies.
-ll-

Take "The Avengers", 	for example.
Easily the highest grossing movie of 2012.
And, 	in fact, 	one of the highest grossing flicks of all time.
But guess what, 	spelling Nazi?
It has more MISTAKES than any other movie this year.
Some anal retentive fanboys 	(the comic book movie equivalent of you
spelling Nazis) 	actually wasted time putting a long list together of
all its continuity mistakes, 	flaws and problems. 	Yet, 	despite them all."
Avengers has made more money than these critics, 	their friends and all
their ancestors to the lOth generation have earned combined.
I think I 	get it, 	though.
Why you're such spiteful pathetic little trolls.
You simply hate selling.
You hate "pitching."
And, 	when you see people making money with 	(GASP!) 	crappy grammar and
language-butchering words." it knocks you off your safe, 	ivory-tower
where you don't actually have to produce anything in order to get golf
claps from your peers.
You can't be a spectator and a player.
It's one or the other.
A player... plays.
And, 	ultimately WINS.
A spectator watches.
And, 	ultimately complains.
In many ways, 	spelling Nazi, 	you're like the stinky slob in the stands
dribbling hotdog mustard on his shirt while yelling at the all-star
shortstop with the 	.300 batting average about how he needs to keep his
head up when swinging the bat 	(even though the slob spectator couldn't
get a single base hit as a kid in little league).

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
said FaceBook was "gay." Whoa! The PC crowd on his list went nutz. ':Jim how can you
make such an evil slur against the gay community!" blah blah blah. And yet, Jim said it
was one of the most profitable emails he'd ever written -- raking in over $5k in sales in a
SINGLE DAY And the beauty of it was, he was smart enough to use the backlash (from a
small minority of his subscribers who never buy from him anyway) to write another
profitable email the next day, explaining how people need to chill out, that saying it was
"gay" is simply in English vernacular these days and deal with it, and it's funny how none
of his gay friends cared and actually thought it was funny, etc. Anyway, let's take a look at
Jim's email and see what made it tick to the tune of $5k...
Subject Line: 	••• and Facebook BLOWS like a radical
What IS it with marketers and this Facebook craze?
What part of "it doesn't work" don't you get?!
Look •••
It's not like I'm one of those people who just hates a thing for the
sake of hating it.
I was one of the first marketers to use Facebook PPC very profitably.
I 	invented the two-step landing page which finally allowed marketers to
get leads from Facebook. 	Before then, 	its PPC was 	just an expensive way
to get "likes".
I even invented a Twitter PPC strategy two years ago which went
seriously viral.
My social media methods are being used today to rank articles on the
front pages of social websites.
I was the one who propagated the Twitter share plugin on Wordpress in
the IM market 	(and even was a code contributor to Tweetmeme years ago -
you'll find a tiny credit to me in the source).
I 	invented the Dynamic Keyword insertion code on landing pages with
PHP.
Trust me, 	if there's a place you can get traffic, 	I've found a way to
make it work.
But for some strange reason, 	I'm an evangelist for Search-Based PPC.

-- 14 of 25 --

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
Ever wonder why?
I'll show you •••
A few months back, we started to promote a WeightLosss eBook on
Facebook.
It's easy enough we thought--plus Facebook is fun, 	and you can target
the "over-weight middle-aged woman" demographic easily.
And although at the time, most of our sales came from our Facebook
traffic, we couldn't get a decent ROI.
Shortly after, we paused our Facebook ads and ran a test for a month
using a single email to a Rented Email list of women. Not overweight
women, 	not middle-aged women ••• 	just "women".
Result?
We broke even in 7-days flat.
we never used Facebook again.
I 	ran a similar experiment with an Internet Marketing product shortly
after.
Facebook converted like crap.
Google Adwords was profitable.
Bottom-line?
Facebook is a SOCIAL environment, 	not a sales one.
So unless you've got big boobs and blonde hair, 	you'll never get your
favourite Social Media "Queen's" results.
I 	know what you want is to get traffic tips about social media ••• but
I'' not going to give them to you.
Wi~h all due respect, 	you aren't the expert on traffic. We are.
So I'll tell you what I 	think you NEED. 	I 	don't want to sell to the
masses ••• I 	just want to sell to the few people who are smart enough to
agree with me: 	Social Media SUCKS for marketing.

Facebook marketing is a waste of time. 	It's gay.
Get on the phone with me tomorrow, 	and I'll tell you what you don't

http://jimyaghi.com/traffic

I think the power behind the email was not the word "gay" (buried all the way down
at the bottom) but the fact the vast majority of people are sick and tired of being censored
for every word, thought and deed these days. You can't breathe a word about anything
without being labeled an "ist" (racist, sexist, misogynist, fascist, name your "ist" ...) And by
being the one lone person out there who speaks your mind, you automatically stick out
like a glowing thumb. Yes, you WILL be attacked and people WILL hurt your feelings.
But do it right, and you'll cry all the way to the bank ...
Word up,

''King 	Of 	Selling 	Online''
21 	Ways 	To 	Be 	lnfotaining
(Part 	3)
Copyright 2012 Ben Settle
In the last 2 parts of this 4 part series 	(taken from a long transcribed
audio training I did for the no-longer-for-sale $795.00 "Street Smart Email"
course) 	we talked about infotainment and how important 	(vital!) 	it is in your
marketing -- especially emails.
This time we're covering the next 8-14 	(of 21) 	ways to use infotainment.
(Infotainment is what I 	consider to be "the king of selling online".)
[START]
Infotainment Secret #8:
Cartoons
Here's a very interesting one, 	because very, 	very, 	very few people do this,
but cartoons always, 	always, 	always get attention, 	probably because they're
so underused and they're kind of expensive, 	and most people think nobody
going's to take a cartoon seriously. When I 	say cartoon, 	I'm talking about a
comic strip kind of cartoon and/or maybe a cartoon caricature.
I 	keep mentioning Matt Furey, 	but he does this. 	He's got this cartoon
caricature of himself like as a super hero. 	There's another guy, 	a sales
genius named Shameless Shamus Brown. 	He does the same thing. 	He's got this
cartoon caricature of himself on his website and it just stands out. 	It's
kind of entertaining. 	It doesn't do anything. 	It doesn't move or anything,
but it's 	just kind of entertaining because it's so different.
Another example would be the old Charles Atlas ads. 	He used to have these ads
in comic books for skinny kids who wanted to get strong. 	There would be this
little comic strip with five panels. 	It would start with this kid getting
sand kicked in his face in front of his girlfriend, 	and then the bully
pushing him down, 	the kid saying, 	"I'm going to get even," and he orders
Charles Atlas' 	Magic Muscle Powder or whatever. 	The next little panel several
weeks later he's big and strong, 	and then the last panel he kicks the bully's
butt and walks away with the girl.
It's short, 	but it sold a ton of that product, 	and you can use this with
almost anything. 	It's almost like the more dry and boring the market, 	the
more effective it'll be. 	It's entertaining, 	it's fun, 	it's perfect
infotainment. 	Especially nowadays on the internet you can have these things
animated.
1
.·. ..:~
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 17 of 25 --

I 	have one client who I mentioned before in the self-defense industry. 	On his
squeeze page he's got an animated cartoon. 	I 	think it only moves like two or
three times, 	but it's some guy attacking him with a knife. 	He turns around,
hits him in the face, 	and it's 	just like an animated cartoon of him and it's
really interesting. 	If you can do something like that, 	you're using really
good infotainment strategy there.
Infotainment Secret #9:
Pop Culture Analogies
As you can tell, 	I'm a big fan of using pop culture. 	Probably just by
listening to this you can tell, 	but pop culture sells big time. 	I mean this
is what sells. 	You might want to call it low culture or whatever you want to
call it, 	but pop culture is what sells.
You can tell if it's pop culture by if it's on TV or that there's a lot of
different magazines on the subject. 	There was a time when everything was
about Britney Spears, 	she was pop culture. 	Nothing you could say about
Britney Spears would not get attention. Everything would get attention about
that.
I'm not picking on her necessarily, 	I'm just saying that pop culture sells.
Anything that's kind of popularly being talked about at any given time, 	and
people already have it on their minds. 	So when you talk about it in your
sales and marketing efforts, 	you're going to kind of borrow that credibility.
Maybe credibility is not the right word. 	You're kind of borrowing that power
that they're already thinking about it and it's already on their mind.
It's very, 	very easy for people to process pop culture analogies. Whenever
you can use something in pop culture as an example to make your point, 	it's
very entertaining.
I'll give you some examples. 	I've done this many times. 	Recently some emails
I 	wrote to my list I 	talked about Mr. 	Furley from Three's Company. 	In
Generation X and older, 	we understand that. 	Maybe the newer generations
don't, 	but most of the people that read my site are in an age bracket that
understand who Mr. 	Furley was. 	He was the bumbling apartment landlord in the
show, 	Three's Company, 	so I 	talked about Mr. 	Furley's sales secrets.
Recently I 	wrote something about Fonzie. 	In fact, 	it was one of those guru
emails I was telling you about, 	where I 	kind of poked fun at the gurus,
saying the real gurus are kind of like Fonzie. 	They don't have to brag about
how many cars they have and all that. 	They're just cool. 	You just know it
just by the way they conduct themselves, 	and that sort of thing. 	So I 	brought
in Fonzie, 	another pop culture icon.
Finally, 	I 	once talked about George Costanza from Seinfeld. 	Even though that
show has been off the air for like 11 years now, 	it's still very popular.
It's on a lot of the cable channels and that sort of thing, 	and everyone just
knew who I was talking about when I brought up George Costanza.
In fact, 	some guy even blatantly ripped off the story I 	told using George
Costanza. 	I 	don't recommend people do that, 	but it does show you the power of
how effective it is. 	People kind of 	just understand it right off the bat.
You can also use pop culture if you're doing a written sales letter. 	I did
this recently with my self-defense client. He has a product to show people
2
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 18 of 25 --

how cops and SWAT teams fight and the stuff that they use when they get
attacked on the street, 	so I 	told a story about a Barney Fife type character.
I mean I 	used his name in it. 	I didn't talk about Barney Fife, 	I 	just said in
the headline about how even skinny Barney Fife-looking cops protect
themselves, 	and that sort of thing.
I 	used a story about a skinny cop who got caught in a mob of angry
protestors, 	and he was skinny, 	kind of a Barney Fife-looking guy, 	and people
just instantly understand that. 	I 	don't have to say how skinny the guy is. 	I
don't have to go into any detail. 	I 	just kind of say that and use pop culture
to my advantage.
Another example is one of my colleagues who writes emails to his list in a
different market, 	and he used Al Bundy from that show, 	Married with Children,
another pop culture icon for certain age groups. 	He said he used that very
successfully. 	He didn't give me exact numbers, 	but he said his sales just
went through the roof when he kind of worked Al Bundy into his story.
I 	do want to make one thing clear here. 	You don't want to break any copyright
or trademark laws here, 	so if you ever have a question about that or a doubt,
just don't do it if you're uncomfortable. 	I'm not advocating using
trademarked things necessarily, 	I'm just saying that you can use pop culture
to your advantage.
Finally, 	there's classic cartoons. 	Talk about classic cartoons to a certain
age group - 	I would say Generation X and older, 	but this might apply to
everybody - 	and it kind of makes people think about their childhood. 	It
almost brings back good memories of when they were just kids without worries
in the world .and they watched cartoons.
You can just name off cartoons. 	I 	once wrote an email about selling a
copywriting book, 	and I 	used Elmer Fudd in it. 	I was talking about the
economy, 	how last year we had this big bank problem and how most people were
just kind of like Elmer Fudd. 	They didn't realize they were walking on air
until they looked down, 	and then they fell.
People just got it. 	The response to that was awesome, 	and I've done that with
other cartoon characters. 	There's this cartoon character from the old
Underdog show, 	called Commander McBragg. 	I 	used him to tell a story once to
make an example.
You can do that and it's very entertaining. 	Believe me, 	there's so many ways
to use pop culture analogies. 	I'm just going over the ones I've used. 	Believe
me, 	there are a ton of them. 	You just want to tap into it whenever you can.
Infotainment Secret #10:
Historical Analogies
This is very, 	very big. 	If you're a history buff this will be easier for you
than if you're not, 	but either way I'm going to tell it to you anyway.
Realize there's nothing new under the sun, 	as the saying goes. 	History really
does repeat itself.
In most cases, 	whatever your market is going through, 	you can give them a
historical example of other people who went through the same thing. 	If
possible, 	hopefully the people who went through it before used a solution
similar to what you're selling.
3
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 19 of 25 --

People can relate to that. 	It's very easy to understand. 	It's interesting. 	I
mentioned the show, 	Smallville, 	earlier. 	Two characters in that show are Lex
Luther, 	who's the main Superman villain, 	and his dad, 	Lionel Luther, 	and
they're always giving these sort of historical analogies whenever they want
to explain something. 	It's kind of weird, 	but it's very entertaining, 	very
infotaining. 	It's 	kind of interesting to hear something put in historical
context.
For example, 	they would say, 	"Did you ever hear the story of Samson?" and
then they would tie it into whatever the plot was of the show, 	but you can do
that when you're selling too. 	I did it with that self-defense client I 	keep
talking about. 	These are just fresh in my mind, 	that's why I 	keep bringing
them up, 	but he was selling a product on wrestling and grappling and that
sort of thing.
It's not the most sexy topic in the world for the people he's selling to, 	so
what I did was I 	found interesting historical 	facts about grappling, 	like how
there were certain gladiators in ancient Rome who used to use grappling to
fight off the blood-thirsty lions when they were thrown into the Coliseum
bare handed. 	They would use grappling. 	So would the ancient Samurai, 	who were
around during the Middle Ages. 	On the battlefield people were kind of scared
to be taken prisoner by these guys, 	because they knew grappling - how to
break bones and that sort of thing.
I 	used these historical examples and it made it a lot more interesting than
saying, 	"Here's a course on grappling." Instead of doing that, 	I 	romanced it
a little. 	I 	told historical analogies and examples.
By the way, 	if you can mix historical analogies with humor and pop culture,
it's even better. 	It's amazing when you start mixing all these sort of
strategies we're going through, 	if you can mix and match them, 	as I 	kind of
suggested from the beginning, 	you'll find that it's much more powerful.
Infotainment Secret #11:
Predictions
There is big money in fortune telling. Now I'm not advocating it, 	I'm not
into that stuff, 	personally I want nothing to do with psychics and all that,
but there's big money in that and you can use it to your advantage without
having to partake in it, 	so to speak. 	That is when you kind of give
predictions about things that are important to your market.
You see this a lot in the financial 	arena. 	You've got people making stock
predictions, 	or political pundits are always predicting things. 	If you can
make a prediction, 	kind of be like a fortune teller in your market - 	I'm not
saying getting all weird and mystical about it, 	I'm just saying making a
prediction - 	you will find that it gets a lot of attention. 	People find it
very entertaining to listen to, 	it's infotainment. 	Even if they don't agree
with you, 	they're going to find it interesting. 	They'll want to participate.
This is especially good if you're using blogs or some kind of interactive way
of selling. 	It's 	just fun to kind of participate in that sort of thing. 	In
fact, 	there's a direct marketing master named Dan Kennedy, 	who a lot of
people in the marketing and copywriting world would know. 	He's like a
household name.
4
.•	www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 20 of 25 --

He wrote this book called The Ultimate Sales Letter, 	and that's one of the
ways that he recommends writing sales letters, 	is to kind of be a fortune
teller. 	When you can predict things and tie it into what's important to your
market, 	it's very, 	very interesting. 	It's entertaining, 	it's infotaining.
By the way, 	you might be saying, 	~well, what if I'm wrong? What if my
prediction falls apart?" You've got to remember, 	we live in a society now
where there's 	just not a huge big fallout if you're wrong about predictions.
I mean if you look at most of the most popular political pundits especially -
and I 	just use them as an example, 	but any type of prediction-type market,
politics or whatever -most of these people are wrong more than they're
right, 	or they seem to be.
If I had to guess, 	and this is just a guess, 	I 	would say even the best are
wrong 50% of the time and nobody's holding it against them. 	It's just
interesting. 	It's just a way to get interest.
I'm not saying to tie your whole business around predictions. 	I 	wouldn't do
that, 	but you can do a very, 	very good job of getting people's attention and
entertaining them at the same time and getting them kind of involved in what
you're saying with predictions.
Infotainment Secret #12:
Goof off
That's right, 	goof off! 	I'm advocating to goof off, 	Let me tell you about the
court jester effect. 	You know what a court 	jester is. 	They were called the
royal fool. 	They were like the person in the royal court dressed all goofy
and kind of dancing around, 	doing back flips, 	acting like an idiot, 	getting
things thrown at them, 	getting kicked around, 	that sort of thing - 	or at
least that's the popular image of it.
But if you really look at it on a deeper level, 	these guys were highly
trusted. 	They got away with saying things about the king and the queen, 	or
joking about people that no one else would dare joke about or they'd probably
lose their head. 	They got away with that sort of thing.
In some cases, 	from what I 	understand - 	and I did some study on this once -
some of these jesters actually became very trusted and valuable confidantes
in the court. 	People actually took their advice and they liked them. 	It was
their job, 	like comedians today, 	to kind of keep their fingers on the pulse
of the political and social atmosphere.
My point is that it's okay to be kind of a goof off. 	I'm not saying do it all
the time, 	but it's okay if you're kind of regularly goofing off. 	You kind of
get away with saying things that you wouldn't normally get away with if you
were known as someone who's just a pure professional. 	There's a certain
expectation on you in that case, 	but it's kind of an entertaining guy or
girl.
You're kind of goofing off and you'll get away with saying things about
whatever. 	You can make certain claims that might sound crazy if anybody else
made it, 	but it'll be okay if you make it because you have that sort of air
about you.
Let's face it, 	people who are goofing off - 	if you're doing it with good
taste - 	tend to be a bright spot right now. 	There's a lot of dark stuff going
5
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 21 of 25 --

on these days. 	I mean there probably always has been, 	but we kind of see this
in the context of the times we're living in now. 	There's a 	lot of pain and
misery out there.
If you can be that bright spot every now and then, 	the person that makes
people laugh - 	we talked about that - 	the person who makes people smile,
that's good. 	That's going to bond you with people. 	People are going to like
you more. 	They're going to trust you more, 	and they're probably going to buy
from you more.
So being kind of a goof off once in a while - 	and again, 	I'm not saying do
this every day, 	I'm just saying you can do it - 	kind of lets you be that
bright spot, 	and people feel 	like they can relate to you. 	You're kind of un-
okay. 	This is something that you'll read about in the book, 	Crackerjack
Selling Secrets. 	You can be un-okay. 	Don't be perfect.
If you're goofing off, 	you're definitely not perfect and you're not even
trying to be. 	It makes people just sort of relate to you. 	They feel 	like they
can buy from you and trust you better.
Let's face it, 	when you do this once in awhile, 	it's kind of a contrast from
what other people are doing. 	Most people really are robotic and cold and
stuffy and they have no personality. 	They're almost like zombies just out
there pumping out content that's 	just try. 	They're afraid to be themselves at
all. 	This kind of cuts right through that.
I have some examples from this from where I've goofed off. 	One example would
be I 	once wrote an email where I 	said why I 	pee on my sales letter, 	as in
urinate is what it seems like. 	That's not what I was talking about, 	but it's
what it looks like on the first glance in the subject line of the email. 	The
point is it was kind of goofy.
Now in the email it was 	just an acronym for persuade, 	engage, 	and entertain.
That's all that meant, 	so it wasn't raunchy or anything, 	but it was kind of
goofy at first glance and it got a lot of attention. 	I mean most of it was
good attention. 	There were a few people out there who didn't appreciate that
and kind of freaked out and all that. 	I don't know why. 	Some people are just
like that, 	but they're not my prospects anyway so I'm not afraid to goof off
like that now and then.
Gary Halbert, 	who I 	mentioned earlier and I 	told you about his website, 	a lot
of his newsletters, 	if you read them, 	he goofed off a 	lot. 	I've seen talk
show hosts and sometimes these guys 	just get flat-out goofy. 	They're just not
even trying to say anything serious or anything, 	and it just comes off kind
of funny.
Comedians would be another one. 	Look how much money people pay comedians, 	and
what are they doing? They're just being paid to basically entertain and goof
off and that's 	it. 	They use some of the infotainment secrets we've been
talking about today, 	but basically comedians are perfect examples of this.
They're almost like today's versions of the royal fool, 	and people like them.
People love their favorite comedians, 	and there's a lot of money associated
with their favorite comedian. 	I 	lot of DVDs are sold, 	and COs and that sort
of thing.

www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 22 of 25 --

Infotainment Secret #13:
Eccentricity
People like the eccentric. 	There's something about the eccentric person in a
group that just draws people to them. 	I don't fully understand it, 	but maybe
it's because it's because they stick out like a sore thumb basically.
When I 	say eccentric, 	I'm talking about like the crazy old uncle in the
corner at the family reunion. 	The guy's like in a rocking chair just telling
bizarre stories. 	None of the adults really like the guy, 	but all the kids
like him and he's the most popular guy at the show, 	and it kind of makes all
the other parents mad. 	He's 	just like that crazy old black sheep uncle.
The fact is, 	a lot of people admire and are actually attracted to the
eccentric. A lot of people wish they were eccentric. 	They want to kind of be
that person who kind of exists outside of rules and does their own thing.
They're kind of like a rebel out there and they're their own person, 	and
there's some kind of freedom attached to that.
There's a lot of deep psychological stuff going on here, 	but the fact is a
lot of people are attracted to that and it lets you get away with doing
things that might normally hurt your credibility, 	because you're already
known as being eccentric, 	so you can say things that go against the rules.
I 	can go around, 	for example, 	and I 	can tell you teaching is not the best way
to sell, 	but if you were to go to certain marketing gurus, 	they'd probably
brand me with something like a letter, 	like in The Scarlet Letter or
something, 	and banish me away for saying something like that, 	but I 	do this
all the time. 	There's nothing wrong with it, 	because I've kind of gotten the
reputation as an eccentric in that sense, 	and you should too. 	I 	highly
suggest it.
I'm not saying you have to go crazy with it and be really weird or anything,
but it's okay. 	You'll get away with saying certain things and people will
take you more seriously when you do step outside and tell people that
breaking the rules is okay, 	so you can sell them something that shows them
how to do whatever it is that you sell. When you have that eccentric quality
to you, 	people will accept it more.
This is especially true when you're dealing with a market that's being
marketed to mostly by so-called professionals and stiff people. 	The more that
you can stick out from them- and by being eccentric, 	that's a good way to do
it - 	the better. Again, 	you can mix this with all the other things we've been
talking about and the next few secrets we'll be talking about.
It's a very interesting thing to kind of take on a crazy old uncle in the
corner persona, 	and I bet you'll see some really good things happening.
Infotainment Secret #14:
Off-the-wall analogies and metaphors
This is especially true on the internet. 	Everything is so benefit-driven -
how to do X, 	how to do Y, 	how to do Z -it's all benefit-driven. 	It's almost
like everybody is 	just trying to shout a bigger, 	stronger benefit to the
market.
7
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 23 of 25 --

You can kind of get around that by using off-the-wall examples and leading
with off-the-wall analogies and examples when you're selling. 	It's a good
contrast to this whole benefit-driven thing, 	and it makes it fun for people.
Again, 	this 	is entertainment. Anything that's different, 	unpredictable, 	and
interesting- even if it's a 	little weird and a little bizarre- people find
it very entertaining, 	or as we've been saying, 	infotaining.
I'm going to give you a couple examples of this so you know what I'm talking
about. 	I 	use emails a lot as my examples, 	because that's my favorite sales
medium, 	but you can use this for 	just about anything, 	so don't think you only
have to do this with email. 	I 	just use that because that's my favorite tool,
but you can apply this with anything that you're selling with.
I 	wrote this email that I 	called "Phantom Pooping Prospects." Now let's face
it, 	that's kind of an off-the-wall thing. 	I mean what the heck does that
mean? Basically what I did is I 	told the story of my dog.
She's kind of a stubborn dog, 	like some dogs are, 	and when you take her out
to do her i2, 	a lot of times she pretends like she has to go out and she
really doesn't. 	She just wants to go sniffing around, 	which isn't the end of
the world or anything, 	but it does get kind of annoying when it's raining out
or something.
Basically, 	what she'll do when it's raining out and she wants to keep us out
there longer is she'll act like she's doing her duty, 	only to not do her duty
and to start sniffing around again. 	This will go on for like 20 minutes and
you're out there soaking wet, 	so I 	called it phantom pooping. 	She's acting
like it, 	but she's not really doing it.
Then I made an analogy like that's how a lot of people buying are. 	A lot of
buyers are like that. 	I'm like that, 	we're all like that, 	where we don't
really buy until we're really ready to buy, 	and until we're ready to buy
we're kind of like dogs out there sniffing around for the perfect spot. 	Then
when we find that perfect spot we'll finally go.
I basically related that to sales. An easy way to sell is to wait until
people are really ready to buy. 	Until then, 	let them sniff around with the
competition, 	but when they're ready to go you make sure you're the one taking
them on.
Anyway, 	that's kind of an off-the-wall analogy and it got a ton of feedback,
and some sales came out of that. 	You can do the same thing. 	You just come up
with these kind of off-the-wall analogies and metaphors. 	Usually from real
life you'll find these if you're just looking around.
Another example is what I 	call Bizarro World marketing. 	Bizarro is this
character in Superman. As you can see, 	I 	use a lot of pop culture in my own
sales. 	He does everything opposite of Superman, 	so up is down, 	down is up,
right is left, 	green kryptonite makes him stronger, 	that sort of thing.
I 	told the story of how in the town I 	live in there's some Bizarro marketing
going on, 	where people almost do the exact opposite of what they should be
doing. 	They give crappy customer service, 	they don't know how to sell, 	they
don't try to market themselves, 	that sort of thing, 	and it got the point
across and it definitely got a lot of response.
8

-- 24 of 25 --

.. ' ~·'
Maybe you're thinking at this point, 	"How do I 	come up with these things? You
know, 	Ben, 	I don't live that interesting of a life and I 	just don't pay
attention to things the way maybe you do." Well, 	you don't have to.
For example, 	the Bizarre World one I got from an episode of Seinfeld, 	which
anyone could probably find on TV, 	no matter where you're at these days, 	which
we talked about earlier. 	That show was brilliant at this. 	Every episode had
some kind of interesting analogy like that.
In this case I was watching an episode where it was Bizarro World Jerry.
Elaine, 	one of the friends, 	kind of met these other three guys that were like
the exact opposite of Jerry, 	George, 	and Kramer.
If you've never seen the show, 	that's fine. 	Just put this on the shelf for
now. The point is, 	that's where I got it from. 	I didn't observe that in real
life, 	but I merged it with something in real life. 	The idea carne from
Seinfeld.
I 	would say if you want to get good at this part of infotainment where you're
starting to use off-the-wall analogies and metaphors, 	turn on Seinfeld and
just watch it. 	You'll get so many ideas you won't even know where to begin.
Just write them down and you'll have a whole list of ideas to work with.
[END)
